frames.caption: Walk the list with prev (behind) and curr (current). prev starts null, curr at the head node 1.
frames.note: Reversing in place means re-pointing every node's `next` from its successor to its predecessor. Two pointers are enough: curr is the node we are rewiring, prev is everything already reversed behind it. O(n) time, O(1) extra space.

frames.caption: Flip 1.next to point back at prev, then advance. The reversed prefix now ends at 1; curr = 2.
frames.note: The one mandatory move: SAVE curr.next into a local before overwriting curr.next = prev — otherwise you lose the rest of the list (the #1 interview bug). After the flip, prev and curr both step forward by one. prev always points at the head of the already-reversed prefix, so when curr falls off the end, prev is the new head.

frames.caption: Flip 2.next to point back at prev, then advance. The reversed prefix now ends at 2; curr = 3.
frames.note: The one mandatory move: SAVE curr.next into a local before overwriting curr.next = prev — otherwise you lose the rest of the list (the #1 interview bug). After the flip, prev and curr both step forward by one. prev always points at the head of the already-reversed prefix, so when curr falls off the end, prev is the new head.

frames.caption: Flip 3.next to point back at prev, then advance. The reversed prefix now ends at 3; curr = 4.
frames.note: The one mandatory move: SAVE curr.next into a local before overwriting curr.next = prev — otherwise you lose the rest of the list (the #1 interview bug). After the flip, prev and curr both step forward by one. prev always points at the head of the already-reversed prefix, so when curr falls off the end, prev is the new head.

frames.caption: Flip 4.next to point back at prev, then advance. The reversed prefix now ends at 4; curr = null.
frames.note: The one mandatory move: SAVE curr.next into a local before overwriting curr.next = prev — otherwise you lose the rest of the list (the #1 interview bug). After the flip, prev and curr both step forward by one. prev always points at the head of the already-reversed prefix, so when curr falls off the end, prev is the new head.

frames.caption: curr is null — every link has been flipped. prev (node 4) is the new head.
frames.note: The loop ends exactly when curr walks off the end. Because prev trailed one step behind the whole way, it is left pointing at the last node visited — which is now the front of the reversed list. Return it as the new head.
